context: Context: Campus and Workplace Small Groups (Philippines)
icebreaker_text: Take 1-2 minutes and have everyone share one weird or funny thing they are secretly proud of. It could be anything from being able to sleep through five alarms, to knowing all the choreography of a random 2000s song, or making the best instant pancit canton!
big_idea: True greatness isn't about flexing what we have, but admitting how much we need Jesus.
key_verse: For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the one who humbles himself will be exalted.
section1_title: The Phony Pharisee and the Truth of the Tax Collector
section1_content:
Jesus tells a striking story in Luke chapter eighteen about two very different men who went to the temple to pray. One was a religious leader who proudly listed his good deeds, thanking God he was better than others. The other was a corrupt tax collector who stayed in the back, too ashamed to even look up. He simply begged for mercy because he knew he was deeply flawed. Jesus shocked His listeners by declaring that the despised tax collector went home right with God. This completely flips the script on how we normally view righteousness. God does not look at our impressive religious flexes; He looks at our willingness to admit our desperate need for Him. As Romans 3:23 reminds us, everyone has sinned and fallen short, making us all totally equal before God.

It is super easy to act like that Pharisee in our hyper-connected world today. We live in a culture that heavily rewards people who brag and build up massive personal brands online. We scroll through our feeds and constantly compare our grades or job titles with our batchmates. Sometimes we even look down on others who are struggling, secretly thinking we are doing much better than them. We start believing our worth comes from what we achieve instead of who created us.

So how do we drop the Pharisee act and adopt the tax collector's humility on campus or at work? Start by being brutally honest with yourself and with God about your own weaknesses. The next time you catch yourself feeling superior to a classmate or coworker, hit pause and pray for them instead. Choose to celebrate the wins of others rather than seeing everyone as your competition. When you finally let go of the exhausting need to prove yourself, you will experience genuine peace.
section1_question:
In what ways do you see people flexing or trying to prove themselves today, both online and in real life?

Paano natin maiiwasan yung trap ng pagiging feeling better than others when we achieve something great?
section1_key_truth: "The Lord is righteous in all his ways and kind in all his works." (Psalm 145:17, ESV)
section2_content:
You might think that hanging out with Jesus every single day would cure anyone of pride. Yet, even Christ's closest disciples constantly argued about who among them was the greatest. They walked with Him, saw His miracles, and witnessed His deep humility, but their selfish ambition still got the best of them. They wanted the highest positions and the best recognition in the kingdom they thought Jesus was building. This shows us that pride is incredibly deeply rooted in our human nature. It is a massive blind spot that offends God because it makes us totally self-sufficient. In Proverbs 16:18, we are warned that pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall. If the disciples struggled with big egos, we need to recognize that we are definitely not immune to this spiritual disease.

Think about your current barkada, your org, or your team at work. It is super common to see subtle power struggles where everyone wants to be the star player or the favorite employee. We often want our ideas to be picked and our efforts to be publicly praised by the boss or professor. When someone else gets the spotlight, jealousy can quickly creep in and ruin our relationships. We mistakenly think that lifting others up will somehow push us down.

Overcoming this competitive mindset requires a massive shift in how we view success. We need to intentionally ask God to reveal the hidden pride in our hearts. Instead of fighting for the top spot, try looking for ways to support your groupmates behind the scenes. Let us practice stepping back so that others can step forward and shine. God promises to lift us up at the right time when we choose to serve silently.
section2_question:
Why do you think the disciples still struggled with pride even though they literally lived with Jesus?

Have you ever experienced a time na sobrang competitive ng environment mo, and how did it affect your heart?
section2_key_truth: "As iron sharpens iron, so one person sharpens another." (Proverbs 27:17, ESV)
section3_content:
In stark contrast to the disciples fighting for power, Jesus perfectly demonstrated what true humility looks like. He gave up the absolute majesty of heaven to become a servant on a broken earth. He did not demand VIP treatment, wealth, or earthly fame during His ministry. Instead, He spent His time healing the sick, feeding the hungry, and washing His followers' dirty feet. His ultimate act of selflessness was dying a humiliating death on the cross to save humanity. Philippians 2:8 beautifully explains that being found in human form, He humbled Himself by becoming obedient to the point of death. Jesus did not use His divine power to elevate Himself; He used it entirely to lift us up.

This kind of radical humility is so rare in our modern society. We are constantly taught to prioritize self-care, self-love, and self-promotion above everything else. Being a servant is usually seen as a sign of weakness or a lack of ambition in the corporate world. People will tell you to step on others if it means getting that promotion or passing a difficult class. But the Jesus-way of living is completely upside-down compared to the world's hustle culture.

To grow closer to God, we need to spend serious time looking at the life of Jesus. As we focus on His pure character, our own pride naturally starts to fade away. Try to adopt His servant mindset in your daily routine this week. Look around your campus or office for someone who is being ignored or overwhelmed. Be the person who offers help without expecting any shoutouts or recognition in return. When we make less of ourselves, we make more room for Jesus to work through us.
section3_question:
How does looking at the life of Jesus change the way we view our own achievements and status?

Ano yung isang practical way na pwede mong ipakita ang "servant mindset" ni Jesus sa school or sa trabaho this week?
section3_key_truth: "Therefore, as God's chosen people, holy and dearly loved, clothe yourselves with compassion, kindness, humility, gentleness and patience." (Colossians 3:12, ESV)
key_insight: We all mess up and let pride take over sometimes, and honestly, that is just part of being human. But the beautiful truth is that God's grace is always bigger than our biggest egos. When you feel exhausted from constantly trying to prove your worth to everyone around you, remember that Jesus already settled your value on the cross. You do not need a perfect feed, top honors, or an impressive job title for Him to love you deeply. Rest in the assurance that you are fully accepted by God right now, just as you are. Let His unconditional love soften your heart, wash away the pressure, and gently guide you into a life of genuine humility.
action_step: Send an encouraging message or give a sincere compliment to a classmate or coworker you usually view as competition. Let them know you appreciate their hard work, and intentionally celebrate their success without mentioning your own.
struggles_list:
Felt like a failure
Made a big mistake
Drifted from prayer/Bible reading
Hurt someone I care about
Gave in to temptation
Doubted God's goodness
safespace_list:
Listen without interrupting
No gossip rule
Share our own struggles first
Pray for each other regularly
Check in during the week
Celebrate small wins together
